title: SEGMENT 8 — Read length, throughput, error modes: Illumina's strengths and limits

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Read length, throughput, error modes: Illumina's strengths and limits
Let me now try to give you an honest, calibrated picture of what Illumina sequencing actually produces — what's good about it, what's not, and where the limits are.
Because there's a lot of marketing around sequencing platforms, and it's easy to come away with either an inflated or a deflated sense of what these machines do.
Read length.
Illumina's defining strength has always been throughput.
Its defining limitation has always been read length.
The longest reads Illumina produces in routine use today are 300 base pairs per end — so 600 bp total per paired-end fragment, with a gap in the middle if the insert is longer.
Compare that to Sanger sequencing, which produces single reads of about 800 to 1000 bp.
Compare it to long-read platforms like Pacific Biosciences and Oxford Nanopore, which we'll meet in Segment 16 — those routinely produce reads of 10,000, 30,000, sometimes over a million base pairs.
Illumina's reads are short.
There's no getting around it.

The chemistry doesn't scale gracefully past a few hundred bases per cycle because errors compound, and a thing called dephasing — which we'll cover in Segment 14 — gradually degrades the signal as the cycles progress.
Why is short-read length a problem?
Mainly for two reasons.

First, repeat regions — places in the genome where the same short sequence is repeated dozens or hundreds of times in a row, like the centromeres of chromosomes, or the tandem repeats associated with diseases like Huntington's.
If your read is shorter than the repeat, you can't tell where in the repeat your read came from, and you can't tell how long the repeat is.
Illumina reads simply cannot resolve a repeat region longer than the read length.
Second, de novo assembly — when you don't have a reference genome and you're trying to piece together the sequence from scratch.
Short reads make assembly hard, because every repeat in the genome becomes an ambiguous junction in the assembly graph.
The original human genome was assembled from much longer Sanger reads partly for this reason.
Modern de novo assembly of a new organism's genome usually combines Illumina short reads (for accuracy) with long-read data from PacBio or Nanopore (for spanning repeats).
Long-read platforms exist precisely because of these failure modes of short reads.
Throughput.
Here's where Illumina is unbeatable.
A NovaSeq X at full configuration produces about 16 terabases of data per run, in roughly a day to a day and a half.
That's about five thousand human genomes' worth of data per machine per run.
A single NovaSeq X facility can sequence twenty thousand human genomes a year.
That throughput — the sheer number of base pairs per dollar, per day, per machine — is what makes population-scale genomics possible.

Projects like the UK Biobank, which is sequencing half a million genomes; the All of Us Research Program in the United States, which is sequencing a million; large clinical implementations like national newborn screening programs — none of these would be financially possible without Illumina-scale throughput.
For smaller-scale work, Illumina also makes smaller instruments.
A MiSeq, the workhorse for small labs, produces a few gigabases per run — enough for a few small bacterial genomes, or a focused panel, or a low-throughput RNA-seq experiment.
A NextSeq sits in the middle — a few hundred gigabases per run, enough for a few dozen exomes or a hundred-sample transcriptome study.
There's an instrument scaled to almost every project size.
We'll cover the instrument family in Segment 9.
Error modes.
Illumina sequencing has an overall error rate, in the well-behaved middle of a read, of about one error per thousand bases — what we call a Q30 quality.
(Quality scores get a whole segment, Segment 12.)
This is excellent.
Other technologies — Oxford Nanopore in particular — have much higher raw error rates, in the range of one error per fifty bases or worse, though they've improved enormously in recent years.
Illumina's per-base accuracy is one of the major reasons it dominates clinical sequencing and variant calling.
But — and this matters — the errors Illumina makes are not random.
They have specific patterns.
The dominant error mode is substitution errors, where the wrong base is called in place of the correct one.
Insertions and deletions are rare in Illumina data, which is excellent for variant calling.
Errors are more common at the 3-prime end of reads — the bases read last — because of the dephasing problem.
Errors are more common in homopolymer runs — long stretches of the same base — though much less so than in some competing platforms.

Errors are biased toward particular base substitutions; one common pattern is that G-to-T substitutions are over-represented because of damage to the DNA during library prep, particularly oxidative damage.
Errors are also concentrated in regions of extreme GC content — very high or very low GC — because these regions don't amplify well during bridge amplification and PCR steps.
Understanding the error profile is critical for variant calling.

When you see a single read with a particular variant, it might be a real biological variant, or it might be a Illumina error — and the probability of each depends heavily on which type of variant it is, where in the read it appears, what the surrounding sequence context is.
Variant callers like GATK and DeepVariant have been trained on millions of Illumina runs to know these patterns and adjust the calling accordingly.
A modern Illumina variant call is, statistically, extremely reliable when the right tools are used.
So the calibrated picture.
Illumina: short reads, around 150 to 300 bp, paired-end.
Phenomenal throughput, billions to tens of billions of reads per run.
Excellent per-base accuracy, around one error per thousand, with specific error patterns concentrated at read ends and in extreme-GC regions.
Bad at long repeats, bad at de novo assembly of repeat-rich genomes, bad at directly reading methylation or other modifications without special chemistry.
Excellent at variant calling, at expression profiling, at any application where having millions of accurate short reads matters more than having a few really long ones.
The platform that built modern genomics is, in every sense, the right tool for the most common jobs and the wrong tool for some specific others.
The competition we'll discuss in Segment 16 has carved out niches in exactly those wrong-tool areas.
